trajectory_summary: The call is a clean procedural win — bill collected via a competent three-way Dominion call, appointment booked for the next day — but the energy never moved. The customer entered as a price-shopper and left as a price-shopper. Parker executed the logistics correctly but skipped nearly the entire Verify section, never anchored bill pain, and booked the appointment using the customer's own transactional framing. The sit is real but fragile: a customer who is 'just finding out what it costs' is one competing quote away from a no-show.
outcome_dispute: The appointment is legitimately booked, so a 'booked' outcome tag is defensible. However, the quality of the booking is low — the customer's frame was never shifted, no co-decision-maker was identified, and the button-up was incomplete. If Ion's outcome taxonomy distinguishes 'booked' from 'qualified booked,' this call belongs in the lower tier.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill-flip / objection inversion — converting price-shopping frame into curiosity
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I don't know if I want it or not. I just wanna find out what it cost.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker received the customer's price-shopping frame at the top of the call and reinforced it at the bottom. The schema's objection_inversion move was available twice — at 01:12 when the customer volunteered $300/month, and at 09:28 after the Dominion kWh data came in — and was unused both times. This is the floor's universal coaching gap per the schema, and it's the single move that separates a 'booked price-shopper' from a 'curious prospect.' The manager should walk Parker through both moments and practice the flip direction before his next call.

key_moments.quote: I don't know if I want it or not. I just wanna find out what it cost.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment and it was walked past. The customer has handed the rep a price-shopping frame — which is also a cost-anxiety frame. The schema's objection_inversion move is directly applicable: the flip on 'I just want to find out what it costs' is to redirect toward what the current bill is already costing. Strategic read: rep could have been deliberately deferring the flip until after bill data was in hand. Reactive read: rep said 'No worries' and moved to logistics, with no evidence of a planned later move. The reactive read holds — the flip never came.

key_moments.quote: The the bills average around that about 300 a month.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the first instance of the 'ask + acknowledge + move on' pattern the schema flags as the universal floor failure. The customer volunteers $300/month unprompted. Parker's response is to ask for kWh data — procedurally correct, emotionally inert. The $300 figure is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch and it was treated as a data point to move past.

key_moments.quote: I think the only thing we needed from you, mister Simonson, was your your electric bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker skips from address confirmation directly to bill collection, bypassing the Verify section. No interest-in-solar question, no title/co-owners check, no roof age, no residence length, no prior designs question. Of the eight Verify items, roughly three are captured across the call (address, bill average, utility company). The schema requires 6-of-8 for completion. The co-owners gap is the most consequential miss — if a spouse or adult child is on the title, the appointment is structurally vulnerable.

key_moments.quote: I got it down here for us, mister Simon. So thanks for taking the time. That'll help us get it started for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second instance of the 'ask + acknowledge + move on' pattern. Parker has just received 13 months of kWh data including a January reading of 2,254 kWh and a February reading of 3,498 kWh — the kind of winter-peak data that makes the bill-flip concrete and personal. Instead, he thanks the customer and pivots immediately to booking. The usage data is filed as administrative input. Note: the May and June readings in the transcript appear truncated or garbled by Deepgram transcription ('78' and '26' respectively) and cannot be used as reliable baselines — the winter figures are the reliable anchor points here.
